Ascentia production architecture

Ascentia Compendium

The living build manual for Ascentia systems: native ownership, designer surfaces, data contracts, runtime links, verification, and future phase guardrails.

Generated from Docs/Architecture/AAA_ARPG_Compendium.md on 2026-06-20. 398 anchored sections. Source commit 717b343. Source SHA-256 prefix: 3a583779fd3e.

Need the Editor-facing what/where/why/how map? Open Designer Compendium for designer-owned surfaces, native-only stops, screenshot status, and definite custom tool candidates. Open Tools for editor-external bridge posture around Landscry, Asset Factory, Skin Forge / InterfaceArtForge, Ultra Dynamic Sky, and Fab / Marketplace intake.

#Ascentia AAA Open-World ARPG Architecture Compendium

Status: draft 0.2, 2026-06-20. Scope: living production architecture for how each step of the Ascentia plan should be written, authored, validated, and connected. This is not a per-line code library. It is the near-code design contract that should make per-line implementation obvious.

#0. Purpose

Ascentia is currently a verified single-player vertical slice plus a fast-growing set of native systems. The next danger is not "can the code do one more thing?" The danger is that every new feature becomes a one-off path, every Blueprint surface drifts, every row format forks, and the eventual open world becomes a pile of local exceptions.

This compendium exists to prevent that. It documents how Ascentia systems should exist at AAA scale:

  • what the native system owns;
  • what Designers see in the Editor;
  • what data structures author the content;
  • what runtime contracts connect systems;
  • what telemetry proves the feature works;
  • what validation catches before play;
  • what presentation hooks make the feature feel alive;
  • what future phases must preserve.

The core principle is already present in Docs/Architecture/00_Overview.md: lower tiers provide stable contracts, higher tiers author content and feel. This compendium turns that into a per-system build manual.

https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/procedural-content-generation-overview

  • Epic World Partition:

https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/world-partition-in-unreal-engine

  • Epic Networking and Multiplayer:

https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/networking-and-multiplayer-in-unreal-engine

  • Epic Online Subsystem and Session Interface:

https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/online-subsystem-in-unreal-engine and https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/online-subsystem-session-interface-in-unreal-engine

  • Epic multiplayer testing and debugging:

https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/testing-and-debugging-networked-games-in-unreal-engine

  • Ultra Dynamic Sky Fab listing and documentation:

https://www.fab.com/listings/84fda27a-c79f-49c9-8458-82401fb37cfb and https://www.ultradynamicsky.com/Documentation/V6/6-20

  • Epic atmosphere, clouds, fog, post process, color grading, material

parameter collections, and scalability: https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/sky-atmosphere-component-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/volumetric-cloud-component-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/exponential-height-fog-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/volumetric-fog-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/post-process-effects-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/color-grading-and-the-filmic-tonemapper-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/using-material-parameter-collections-in-unreal-engine https://dev.epicgames.com/documentation/unreal-engine/scalability-reference-for-unreal-engine

#External source takeaways

The key online takeaways we should apply, not merely quote:

  • GAS is specifically built for RPG/action games where abilities coordinate

animation, VFX, stats, costs, cooldowns, interrupts, UI, ownership, and replication. Ascentia should move verbs into ability objects and keep combat calculations as reusable native funnels.

  • Gameplay Attributes and Attribute Sets should own persistent numerical state,

clamping, derived-value reactions, replication notifications, and permanent base-value changes. Ascentia's damage, build, equipment, and save systems should treat Attribute Set values as authoritative facts, not UI-only numbers.

  • Gameplay Effects are the correct unit for stat changes, buffs, debuffs,

periodic effects, stacking, and cue triggers. Ascentia itemization should stop relying on two-value affix enums and move toward modifier-tagged Gameplay Effects and SetByCaller magnitudes.

  • Gameplay Mod Magnitude Calculations and custom GameplayEffect executions are

the production route when damage math needs captured source/target attributes, SetByCaller values, source tags, target tags, and conditional modifiers. The current native damage-packet function is acceptable MVP scaffolding, but the long-term damage model should become a named calculation/execution with transparent telemetry.

  • Gameplay Cues are cosmetic. Gameplay truth belongs in replicated attributes,

tags, telemetry, and ability tasks. Cues are the designer-authored feedback layer, not the gameplay source of truth. They are also the network-efficient way GAS expects cosmetic particles and sounds to be managed.

  • Epic's data-driven gameplay guidance directly supports Ascentia's spreadsheet

path: designers maintain data in Excel or a similar source, export CSV, and import it into row-struct-backed DataTables. Programmers own the row struct; Designers own the row content.

  • Data Registries provide the exact production pattern Ascentia needs: multiple

ordered data sources, read-only data lookup, sync/async access, and fallback or override behavior by source order. Ascentia can keep its item registry as the gameplay facade while shaping it like a Data Registry adapter.

  • Gameplay Tags are hierarchical labels for actions, events, capabilities, and

object traits, and they can be defined in config, C++, or DataTables. Ascentia should use tags as the stable authoring and interop language, not raw strings or one-off enums.

  • Epic's Blueprint/C++ guidance frames the split as logic vs data. Ascentia

should put authority and invariants in native code while leaving authored content, object references, and presentation in DataAssets, DataTables, Blueprint classes, and editor-facing validators.

  • Editor Utility Widgets are dockable editor panels backed by UMG and Blueprint

scripting. Ascentia should use them for rich Designer workflows such as content sync, validation dashboards, map previews, loot simulation, and boss move matrices.

  • Python editor scripting is for production pipeline automation inside the

Editor, not runtime gameplay. Ascentia should use it for batch asset import, naming repair, report generation, and MCP-controlled editor operations, while keeping runtime rules in native/game modules.

  • Scriptable Tools and Editor Utility Widgets split along interaction shape:

EUWs are excellent dockable panels, while Scriptable Tools fit modal viewport interactions such as painting, placement, measuring, or manipulating selected scene geometry.

  • Geometry Script exposes mesh generation and editing through Blueprint and

Python. Asset Factory and pack-ingestion tools should use it for inspection, repair, collision proxy creation, socket marker checks, and modular mesh variation where appropriate, but generated geometry should still pass validation before content is accepted.

  • Blueprint Function Libraries are the correct home for shared Blueprint utility

functions that should not belong to one gameplay object. Ascentia should keep global editor/gameplay helper nodes in libraries and keep actor Blueprints focused on authored content.

  • UPROPERTY specifiers and metadata are part of the product surface:

EditAnywhere, EditDefaultsOnly, BlueprintReadOnly, categories, tooltips, clamping, AssetRegistrySearchable, and SaveGame are how native contracts become usable in the Editor.

  • UMG should use reusable User Widgets and event-driven updates where possible.

Bindings/tick-heavy UI should be avoided for high-frequency production HUD and inventory surfaces.

  • SaveGame is correct for persistent local state, but async save is the

production default for meaningful data because synchronous disk work can hitch the frame and create platform-certification risk. P19 should treat the current synchronous MVP save as a small-slice implementation, not the final pipeline.

  • Loading must use the same slot and user identity used to save. P19 therefore

needs explicit slot headers, local-player ownership, migration, and validation instead of anonymous one-off slot names.

  • CommonUI's input routing exists specifically so layered menus can decide which

widget receives mouse/gamepad input at a given time. Ascentia should use it for inventory, character creation, save slots, settings, and modal prompts instead of relying on ad hoc input-mode toggles scattered across screens.

  • Enhanced Input Player Mappable Input Configs are preset collections of mapping

contexts. P20/P21 should put remapping and input glyphs on that path rather than hard-coding key text in widgets.

  • Niagara is the artist-owned VFX layer. Niagara Data Channels add the scalable

event stream needed for high-volume impacts, footsteps, status bursts, loot beams, and battlefield ambience without spawning a separate bespoke system for every hit.

  • MetaSounds give audio designers a graph-driven DSP authoring layer. Ascentia

should reserve simple USoundBase one-shots for MVP proof and graduate combat, ambience, music state, and boss transitions to profile-driven MetaSound sources where designers need procedural variation and parameter control.

  • Camera Shake Sources and shake assets should be profile-driven, distance-aware,

and accessible through intensity settings. Combat code should emit a fact such as "heavy hit landed"; camera code should decide the shake asset and scale.

  • Ultra Dynamic Sky should be treated as a strong third-party sky/weather

provider, not as the Ascentia architecture itself. Its single time-of-day control, dynamic sky, weather, lighting, and material-weather features should be wrapped by Ascentia profiles, tags, validation, and Designer preview tools so the project can author atmosphere consistently and swap implementation details later if needed.

  • Packaging is a build pipeline, not a final menu click. Epic separates build,

cook, stage, package, deploy, and run, and UAT BuildCookRun is the automation path. P21 should define repeatable package profiles and command-line gates before a "ship" claim is trusted.

  • Automation tests and functional tests are production proof surfaces, not only

programmer tests. Ascentia tool output should feed automated editor/runtime tests where possible, and the dashboard should show the last run and evidence for each feature area.

  • Gauntlet exists for running Unreal sessions and validating results across one

or more clients/servers. Ascentia should graduate remote playtest scenarios into Gauntlet-style packaged smoke when build cadence justifies it.

  • Unreal localization is built around FText, localization targets, string

tables, and the Localization Dashboard. User-facing Ascentia text should never be permanent FString UI copy; P21 needs a text namespace/string-table policy and a pseudo-localization proof pass.

  • Device Profiles and scalability settings are the supported path for

per-hardware quality. Ascentia should keep gameplay settings separate from rendering/performance presets, then map those presets to scalability groups, CVars, and platform/device profiles.

  • Networking is server-authoritative by default, with replication, RPCs,

replicated movement, Online Subsystem sessions, and network testing tools as explicit production surfaces. P22 should mature existing GAS, Mover, replicated inventory/equipment, and world-state seams rather than bolting multiplayer on as a separate game.

  • Open-world objective design needs legible goals without turning the world into

a checklist. Ascentia's "no quest log, world state instead" direction is strong if objective hints, NPC lines, POIs, map clues, and rewards all resolve from the same state graph.

  • System-driven dialogue should be fed by world progress, relationship/state

changes, and player decisions. Ascentia's current FAscentiaDialogueLine requirement/effect tags are the right MVP seed, but production dialogue needs authoring tools, localization, preview, VO/subtitle hooks, and unreachable-line validation.

  • Branching objective content is expensive to repair late. P12 should establish

an iterative authoring loop: stub the state graph, test it in editor, attach dialogue/vendor/tutorial/map presentation, then validate save/load and progression before polishing prose or cinematics.

  • Blizzard's itemization guidance is directly applicable to Ascentia: affixes

should be meaningful parts of character power and should create choices, not merely bigger numbers. P16 should therefore treat modifier tags, affix pools, unique powers, stat requirements, skill interactions, and resource loops as one build language.

  • Blizzard's more recent defense/itemization updates reinforce a second P13

rule: defense math has to be legible. Armor, resistances, damage reduction, healing, quality upgrades, and enemy pressure should expose clear build tradeoffs, diminishing returns, and an effective-survivability view instead of burying power inside invisible multipliers.

  • Action RPG loot sits on a spectrum between set authored rewards and random

generated rewards. Ascentia should intentionally mix both: Soulslike memorable placed/boss/world rewards plus ARPG affix-driven drops, with different proof gates for each.

  • Economy health depends on sources and drains. Stones, materials, items, and

upgrade currencies need explicit faucets, sinks, caps, and conversion paths; otherwise loot becomes clutter and vendors/crafting become irrelevant.

  • World Partition stores the world as one persistent level subdivided into

streaming cells; Data Layers organize and runtime-toggle sets of actors. P17 must adopt this before large-world content is authored in earnest.

  • World Partition HLOD is the large-world visibility/performance layer for

unloaded cells. Ascentia needs HLOD layer rules for terrain, cliff kits, foliage, buildings, POIs, and imported/generated Landscry assets.

  • PCG can inherit World Partition Data Layers and HLOD Layers, and PCG

generation modes can partition generation across grids. Landscry integration should output or be converted into layer-aware, partition-aware content rather than a monolithic dumped level.

  • World Partition builders provide commandlet gates for nav data, smart object

collections, and PCG generation. P17 validation should include builder passes, not just PIE.

  • Asset Manager Primary Assets are the right identity and load boundary for

content that must be discovered, cooked, referenced by stable IDs, and loaded on demand. Ascentia should use PrimaryDataAssets for object-heavy content and DataTables for bulk numeric rows.

  • Large projects need naming conventions early. Ascentia should standardize

prefixes for DataTables, PrimaryDataAssets, GameplayAbilities, GameplayCues, Animation Blueprints, Behavior Trees, Blackboard assets, EQS assets, Widgets, Niagara systems, MetaSounds, and maps before content volume ramps.

  • Data Validation can run in editor and commandlets. Production Ascentia should

fail early on missing icons, meshes, ability classes, cue tags, invalid requirements, broken loot pools, invalid Data Layers, and stale content paths.

  • Mover is promising for rollback/network-aware movement but still experimental.

Ascentia should keep its movement abstraction (FAscentiaCombatMotionRequest, IAscentiaCombatant) instead of letting gameplay depend directly on a single pawn movement implementation.

  • Game Animation Sample proves a modern high-fidelity animation stack uses

motion trajectory, motion matching, root offset, orientation warping, IK, database LOD, debug views, and character overrides. Ascentia should keep GASP integration as the presentation locomotion layer, not a reason to pollute combat system contracts with GASP-specific assumptions.

  • Root Motion is the correct way to let authored combat animations move the

capsule, but it must be enabled per sequence/montage and selected through the Animation Blueprint root-motion mode. UE also applies movement-mode physics: walking/falling characters still receive gravity and handle slopes/stairs.

  • Animation Montages are the right one-shot combat animation container: they can

be controlled from Blueprint, split into sections, and replicate root motion in networked games.

  • Animation Slots are how P14 keeps action playback modular: full-body slots for

committed melee, upper-body slots for aiming/shooting/reload-style actions, and dedicated slots for paired weapons or off-hand overlays where needed.

  • Animation Notifies are the correct designer-authored timing surface for swing

audio, trails, damage windows, cancel windows, iframe windows, projectile release, footstep emphasis, and cue markers. Native code should validate the notifies and consume them through stable events rather than hiding timing in C++.

  • Motion Warping adds named montage windows that can adjust root motion toward

targets through Blueprint logic. Ascentia should use it for target-aligned executions, grabs, mantles, and boss moves, not for ordinary free dodge intent.

  • The Gameplay Targeting System provides data-driven targeting presets built

from selection, filtering, and sorting tasks. Ranged, throwable, spell, lock-on, and future weak-point actions should keep their data compatible with this model even while the MVP uses simpler trace/projectile paths.

  • Character Movement's root motion source system exists for programmatic special

movement such as leaps, dashes, pulls, and skills whose distance/arc is defined by gameplay variables instead of an animator-authored clip.

  • Pose/Orientation Warping can cover animation-direction gaps, but it is

presentation. It should make a selected move look better; it should not decide which dodge direction the player requested.

  • Horizon's AI transition from corridor shooter to open-world action RPG required

new navigation, animation, group coordination, planning, and utility decision systems. Ascentia P18 should expect AI architecture work, not just more enemy Blueprints.

  • EQS is the right tool for spatial decisions: attack positions, retreat points,

cover, flank routes, spacing anchors, and arena positions. Behavior Trees should call EQS queries instead of hardcoding vectors.

  • StateTree is a performant hierarchical state machine that combines behavior

tree selectors with state-machine transitions. Ascentia should consider it for ambient/open-world AI states and Smart Object behavior, while preserving BT/EQS where designers need readable combat branching.

  • Smart Objects are globally queryable, spatially partitioned, tag-filtered

activities with a reservation model. They are the right authoring surface for camps, patrol posts, ambush points, workstations, chairs, ritual sites, doors, lifts, and contextual AI/player interactions.

  • UE's AI debugger can inspect Behavior Trees, EQS, and AI Perception in one

place. Ascentia should design AI proof around visible debug state, not only log counters.

  • Diablo IV's itemization discussion reinforces that affixes must be meaningful

character power and must create real equip choices. Ascentia's affix engine must produce build-defining tradeoffs, not only bigger damage numbers.

  • Elden Ring's open-world lesson for Ascentia is that area design and boss

gameplay are coupled. A zone is not a terrain chunk with enemies sprinkled on it; major enemies, routes, resources, level beats, and reward pressure shape each other.

#2. The Production Contract

Every Ascentia feature must be written in five layers:

  1. Native truth.

The authoritative state, prediction/replication decisions, save ownership, combat math, inventory rules, world-state changes, and validation predicates.

  1. Data truth.

Stable row structs, Primary Asset IDs, GameplayTags, curves, soft references, and import rules.

  1. Designer surface.

Class Defaults, DataAssets, DataTables, UMG hooks, GameplayAbility Blueprints, GameplayCue Blueprints, Behavior Trees, EQS queries, Data Layers, and validation messages.

  1. Presentation surface.

Animation montages, motion matching databases, notifies, Niagara, MetaSound, camera, controller feedback, UI, audio mix, level dressing, and tutorial copy.

  1. Proof surface.

Remote playtest scenario, screenshot/log evidence where useful, commandlet validation, content completeness report, and docs/dashboard sync.

If a phase only does native truth, it is a scaffold. If it only does presentation, it is a prototype. AAA progress means all five layers exist, even if the first slice is thin.

#3. Shared Invariants

These invariants apply to every system.

#3.1 GameplayTags are the boundary language

Enums can exist inside tight native code, but content and cross-system contracts must speak tags. Examples:

  • ability identity: Ascentia.Ability.*
  • moveset identity: Ascentia.Moveset.*
  • weapon type identity: Ascentia.Weapon.Type.*
  • item identity: Ascentia.MVP.Item.* now, later Ascentia.Item.*
  • cue identity: GameplayCue.Ascentia.*
  • status identity: Ascentia.Status.*
  • world-state identity: Ascentia.World.* or validated FName migrated to tags
  • modifier identity: future Ascentia.Mod.*

Rule: if a Designer might author it, filter by it, save it, display it, or bind VFX/audio to it, it gets a tag.

#3.2 Native code owns authority, not authorship

Native code should answer:

  • Is this action allowed?
  • What state changes happen?
  • What data shape is valid?
  • What replicates?
  • What persists?
  • What telemetry proves it?

Native code should not answer:

  • Which sword is cool?
  • Which boss attack looks best?
  • Which hit spark is dramatic enough?
  • Which spell icon reads better?
  • Which chest has the more satisfying reward rhythm?

Those are Designer surfaces.

#3.3 Data-first, Blueprint-second, C++-last authoring

The cheapest stable surface wins:

  • DataTable row for bulk stats.
  • PrimaryDataAsset for content with object references and identity.
  • Blueprint subclass for behavior variation.
  • Native C++ for contracts and hard rules.

If a new feature starts in C++ because the surface does not exist yet, the same change must document the intended Designer surface. "Hardcoded for now" is allowed for a smoke slice, but only when paired with the migration seam.

#3.4 Every "done" claim needs a verifier

For each feature, the proof should include:

  • a remote playtest scenario when runtime behavior matters;
  • a commandlet/build step when content integrity matters;
  • log scan for known regressions when the feature has prior crash/warning history;
  • a dashboard/docs update with status and remaining Designer layer;
  • named evidence paths.

The goal is not bureaucratic paper. It is to keep the project honest when many systems are changing quickly.

#4. The System Template

Each major system section in this compendium should eventually follow this shape:

System name
1. Player-facing goal
2. Current Ascentia state
3. AAA target behavior
4. Native authority
5. Data model
6. Designer surface
7. Presentation hooks
8. System interconnects
9. Save/replication
10. Validation rules
11. Remote-playtest proof
12. Phase ownership
13. Failure modes
14. Future expansion

When implementing a phase, write the system section first or update it before coding. The doc is the contract; the code is the fulfillment.

#5. Foundation: GAS, Attributes, Effects, Tags

#5.1 Player-facing goal

Every action, item, status, spell, passive, enemy move, and build effect should feel systemic. A player should be able to understand that gear, stats, skills, resources, enemy states, and world conditions interact through a coherent ruleset.

#5.2 Current Ascentia state

Ascentia already has the hard part started:

  • UAscentiaAttributeSet with replicated health, breath, eth, poise, primary

stats, derived stats, combat feedback attributes, and meta attributes.

  • UAscentiaGameplayAbility base plus native abilities for dodge, light/heavy

attack, spell cast, weapon skill, Eth potion, and other verbs.

  • UAscentiaCombatComponent as the central combat authority.
  • FAscentiaDamagePacket as the unified damage description.
  • Gameplay tags declared in AscentiaGameplayTags.*.
  • replicated telemetry for combat, status, guard counter, movesets, ranged,

throwable, spell, and boss presentation slices.

The structural gap is that several features still behave as native C++ verbs instead of full ability assets, and itemization still has a narrow affix path.

#5.3 AAA target behavior

The target is not "everything is a GameplayAbility." The target is:

  • player and enemy verbs are ability-driven where timing/cost/cooldown/cancel

matter;

  • damage/status math is centralized and reusable;
  • stat changes are Gameplay Effects;
  • cosmetic feedback is Gameplay Cues;
  • ability identity, cooldown identity, status identity, and cue identity are tags;
  • UI reads replicated state and telemetry, not transient animation state;
  • Designers can create a new skill by authoring data, a GA Blueprint, cues,

animations, and validation-passing assets.

#5.4 Native authority

Native systems should provide:

  • UAscentiaGameplayAbility base behavior:
  • cost resolution: Breath, Eth, charges, ammo, item stack, cooldown;
  • source item context;
  • skill tag;
  • cue tag;
  • telemetry stamp;
  • validation helper.
  • UAscentiaCombatComponent:
  • damage packet construction;
  • damage packet application;
  • defensive state resolution;
  • status buildup and payload application;
  • attack/contact timing handoff;
  • combat motion request emission;
  • replicated read model.
  • UAscentiaAttributeSet:
  • attribute declarations;
  • clamping;
  • derived values;
  • meta attributes.

Native code should not own individual skill flavor beyond fallback test content.

#5.5 Data model

Ability data should converge toward:

FAscentiaSkillDefinitionRow
- SkillTag
- DisplayName
- Description
- Delivery
- ResourceType
- ResourceCost
- CooldownTag
- CooldownSeconds
- DamagePackets or DamageProfileTag
- StatusBuildupTag
- CueTag
- AbilityClass
- RequiredWeaponMovesetTags
- RequiredWorldStateTags
- BlockedStateTags
- UIIcon
- InputHintTag

The PrimaryDataAsset version exists when object references matter:

UAscentiaSkillDefinitionAsset
- Row: FAscentiaSkillDefinitionRow
- AbilityClass
- MontageSet
- Niagara/Cue references if not tag-only
- Sound references if not cue-only
- Designer notes

DataTables should own the bulk numeric shape. PrimaryDataAssets should own content identity and object references. The registry can ingest both.

#5.6 Designer surface

Designers should see:

  • GA_Ascentia_* Blueprint classes derived from native bases.
  • PDA_AscentiaSkill_* assets for skill identity.
  • GameplayCue Blueprints under GameplayCue.Ascentia.*.
  • montage and notify authoring surfaces.
  • Class Defaults for costs, windows, root motion policy, input buffering, and

cancellation.

  • UMG bindings that show current cost/cooldown/resource state.

The surface must be safe:

  • invalid cost tags fail validation;
  • missing cue tags warn;
  • missing montage notifies fail combat-content validation;
  • ability classes that do not derive from UAscentiaGameplayAbility fail;
  • cooldown tags must be stable and namespaced;
  • skill rows must not reference test-only assets for production cook.

#5.7 Presentation hooks

Every ability needs:

  • activation cue;
  • impact cue;
  • whiff or swing cue when relevant;
  • cooldown/ready UI signal;
  • resource failure signal;
  • optional camera/rumble profile;
  • montage notify windows for damage/contact;
  • input buffer/cancel window visualization for debugging.

Gameplay Cues are cosmetic only. If a cue is dropped over the network, gameplay must remain correct.

#5.8 Interconnects

Ability execution touches:

  • Inventory for source item, ammo, quick item, and equipment effects.
  • Progression for stats/passives.
  • Combat for damage and defensive resolution.
  • Avatar component for contact sweeps and weapon presentation.
  • Motion solver for root motion or fallback movement.
  • World state for objective triggers and event gates.
  • UI for cooldown/resource/status feedback.
  • Save for persistent grants, known spells, unlocked skills, and equipped slots.

This is why the ability should be a coordinator, not a blob of math.

#5.9 Verification

Each ability family needs:

  • one remote playtest proving activation, cost, cooldown, telemetry, and rejection;
  • one content validation pass proving required assets exist;
  • one regression pass proving existing light attack/dodge/block/status still work;
  • one docs/dashboard update marking native, Designer, presentation, and content

state separately.

#5.10 Foundation starter implementation package

The foundation package should make Ascentia's ability, attribute, effect, and tag rules explicit before later phases multiply content. This is a cross-phase lane named FND, not a single gameplay phase. Its purpose is to prove that every player verb, enemy verb, item modifier, spell, status, passive, UI query, and network-sensitive gameplay path speaks the same GAS/tag/attribute language.

Reference posture:

  • Ability System Components are the bridge between Actors and GAS. Player-owned

long-lived ability state should be able to live on PlayerState, while pawn or enemy fallback paths can own local ASCs when lifetime is simpler.

  • Attribute Sets own persistent numeric gameplay state and clamping hooks.

Derived UI values can be view models, but source numeric truth should remain in attributes, saved source facts, or resolved content.

  • Gameplay Effects are data assets for modifying attributes, granting/removing

tags, applying ongoing states, and running execution calculations. Do not put gameplay logic in GameplayEffect Blueprint graphs.

  • Gameplay Abilities are the right unit for multi-stage actions with costs,

cooldowns, animation, input branching, targeting, and presentation.

  • Gameplay Tags are the boundary language. Prefer tags over strings, enum drift,

or unrelated booleans when systems need to communicate state or identity.

  • Lyra-style Ability Sets are a useful pattern: one data asset grants abilities,

effects, and attribute sets, with handles tracked for removal.

PDA_AbilitySet_Player_MVP
PDA_AbilitySet_GASPPlayer_MVP

GE_Ascentia_Cost_Eth_EtherSpark_MVP
GE_Ascentia_Cooldown_LightAttack_MVP
GE_Ascentia_Cooldown_Dodge_MVP
GE_Ascentia_Status_Poison_Buildup_MVP

Ability set pattern:

UAscentiaAbilitySet : UPrimaryDataAsset
  GrantedGameplayAbilities
  GrantedGameplayEffects
  GrantedAttributeSets
  StartupTags
  InputBindings
  ValidationPolicy

FAscentiaAbilitySetGrant
  AbilityClass
  AbilityLevel
  InputTag
  ActivationPolicyTag
  SourceTag

FAscentiaAbilitySetGrantedHandles
  GrantedAbilitySpecHandles
  GrantedGameplayEffectHandles
  GrantedAttributeSets
  SourceAbilitySet

Rules:

  • player, enemy, boss, spell, equipment, summon, and temporary buff grants should

all use ability-set or effect-library patterns;

  • every grant must be removable or intentionally permanent;
  • input tags are data, not hardcoded key names;
  • startup effects must say whether they are source facts, derived modifiers, or

prototype fallbacks;

  • ability sets should write a grant report for PIE/package proof.

Tag taxonomy starter:

Ascentia.Ability.*
Ascentia.Action.*
Ascentia.Attribute.*
Ascentia.Combat.State.*
Ascentia.Cooldown.*
Ascentia.Cost.*
Ascentia.Damage.*
Ascentia.Defense.*
Ascentia.Effect.*
Ascentia.Equipment.*
Ascentia.Input.*
Ascentia.Item.*
Ascentia.Motion.*
Ascentia.Presentation.*
Ascentia.Resource.*
Ascentia.Save.*
Ascentia.Spell.*
Ascentia.Status.*
Ascentia.System.*
Ascentia.WorldState.*
GameplayCue.Ascentia.*

Tag registry report:

FAscentiaGameplayTagRegistryReport
  RunId
  TagSources
  TagsDeclared
  TagsReferenced
  MissingTags
  DeprecatedTags
  RedirectedTags
  InvalidNamespaces
  DisplayStringUsageFindings
  DuplicateMeaningFindings
  Errors
  Warnings

Attribute report:

FAscentiaAttributeSetReport
  RunId
  AttributeSets
  Attributes
  ReplicationPolicy
  ClampRules
  DerivedAttributeRules
  SaveSourceFacts
  UIReadModels
  MissingClampRules
  MissingReplicationHooks
  Errors
  Warnings

GameplayEffect library report:

FAscentiaGameplayEffectLibraryReport
  RunId
  EffectsChecked
  CostEffects
  CooldownEffects
  StatusEffects
  PassiveEffects
  ModifierEffects
  ExecutionCalculations
  SetByCallerContracts
  GrantedTags
  RemovedTags
  InvalidBlueprintGraphUsage

Ability grant report:

FAscentiaAbilityGrantReport
  RunId
  AbilitySet
  TargetActor
  ASCActor

  GrantedEffects

  Errors
  Warnings

Commandlets:

AscentiaFoundationAudit
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/Foundation
  -IncludeAbilities
  -IncludeEffects
  -IncludeAttributes
  -IncludeTags

AscentiaGameplayTagAudit
  -TagProfile=/Game/Ascentia/Design/Foundation/Tags/PDA_TagTaxonomyProfile_MVP
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/Foundation/Tags
  -FailOnMissingReferencedTags

AscentiaAbilitySetAudit
  -AbilitySetRoot=/Game/Ascentia/Design/Foundation/AbilitySets
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/Foundation/AbilitySets

Editor Utility Widgets:

EUW_AscentiaGameplayTagTaxonomy
  Shows declared tags, referenced tags, missing tags, redirects, deprecated tags,
  namespace ownership, and display-string branching findings.

EUW_AscentiaAbilitySetBrowser
  Shows abilities, effects, attributes, startup tags, input bindings, grant
  handles, removal policy, and latest grant proof.

EUW_AscentiaGameplayEffectLibrary
  Shows cost/cooldown/status/passive/equipment effects, SetByCaller contracts,
  GameplayCue routes, granted tags, and invalid Blueprint graph usage.

EUW_AscentiaAttributeInspector
  Shows attributes, clamps, replication, derived values, source facts, save
  responsibilities, and UI read models.

Validation:

VLD_Ascentia_GameplayTagTaxonomy
VLD_Ascentia_GameplayTagReferences
VLD_Ascentia_AbilitySets
VLD_Ascentia_AbilityGrantHandles
VLD_Ascentia_GameplayEffects
VLD_Ascentia_GameplayEffectBlueprintGraphs
VLD_Ascentia_SetByCallerContracts
VLD_Ascentia_AttributeSets
VLD_Ascentia_AttributeClampRules
VLD_Ascentia_ASCOwnership
VLD_Ascentia_InputAbilityBindings

Proof matrix:

Foundation_PlayerAbilitySet_GrantsAndRemoves
Foundation_EnemyAbilitySet_GrantsAndRemoves
Foundation_BreathCost_RejectsWhenEmpty
Foundation_EthCost_RejectsWhenEmpty
Foundation_Cooldown_TagBlocksReactivation
Foundation_AttributeClamp_HealthBreathEthPoise
Foundation_StatusEffect_GrantsAndClearsTags

Foundation_GameplayEffect_NoBlueprintGraphs
Foundation_ASCOwnership_PlayerStateAndPawnPaths

FND starter tickets:

  • FND-001: Add UAscentiaAbilitySet, grant structs, handle bookkeeping, and

Player/Enemy/Boss MVP ability-set assets.

  • FND-002: Add Gameplay Tag taxonomy profile, tag audit commandlet, and missing

referenced-tag report.

  • FND-003: Add GameplayEffect library profile and validation for cost,

cooldown, status, passive, SetByCaller, cue, and graph-free effect assets.

  • FND-004: Add attribute report and clamp validation for Health, Breath, Eth,

Poise, primary stats, derived stats, and meta attributes.

  • FND-005: Add ASC ownership report covering PlayerState-owned player ASC,

pawn fallback ASC, CMC enemy ASC, and boss ASC paths.

  • FND-006: Add ability input binding report connecting Enhanced Input actions,

input tags, ability specs, and UI prompts.

  • FND-007: Add Foundation dashboard card and evidence folders.
  • FND-008: Add package gate that blocks missing tags, invalid ability classes,

invalid cost/cooldown effects, and production GameplayEffects with Blueprint graphs.

Recommended implementation order:

  1. FND-001 first. Ability sets become the common grant/removal pattern for

players, enemies, equipment, spells, summons, and bosses.

  1. FND-002 next. Tags are already the boundary language; missing or duplicated

meanings become expensive if left loose.

  1. FND-003 and FND-004 then lock down effect and attribute rules before P13/P15

broaden damage, status, spells, and item modifiers.

  1. FND-005 and FND-006 prove ownership and input binding across GASP pawn,

native pawn, enemy, and boss paths.

  1. FND-007 and FND-008 close the loop with dashboard and package gates.

#6. Combat, Motion, and Animation Intent

#6.1 Player-facing goal

Combat should honor player intent while respecting animation commitment. A player can attack, commit, cancel inside designed windows, dodge in the direction they are asking for, fall off ledges when motion carries them beyond support, move down stairs without false falling, and read every hit, block, parry, whiff, status, and stagger.

#6.2 Current Ascentia state

Current working pieces:

  • FAscentiaDamagePacket carries health damage, poise damage, school, status

buildup, source/target context, and cue identity.

  • UAscentiaCombatComponent::ApplyDamagePacket resolves parry, i-frames, armor,

resistance, block, guard break, status, GAS damage, feedback, and cues.

  • IAscentiaCombatant lets GASP pawn, native Mover pawn, and CMC enemies share

combat.

  • FAscentiaCombatMotionRequest starts separating combat intent from movement

implementation.

  • AAscentiaGaspCharacter currently supports root-motion attack and dodge paths,

floor-loss guard for attacks, directional dodge roll selection, weapon-based attack animation resolution, and Designer-visible dodge/weapon knobs.

  • UAscentiaAvatarComponent owns weapon contact sweep, weapon trail, swing

whoosh, and hit reaction presentation.

Current debt:

  • UAscentiaCombatComponent still has parallel defensive booleans.
  • root-motion and cancel policy are not yet governed by a unified combat motion

solver.

  • attack, dodge, jump attack, rolling attack, guard counter, parry, stagger, and

hit reaction need one shared state/cancel model.

#6.3 AAA target behavior

The target is a combat motion solver:

Input Intent
  -> Ability / combat verb request
  -> Rule check: resource, cooldown, state, cancel window, equipment, surface
  -> Motion plan: root motion, override velocity, constrained root, or fallback
  -> Animation plan: visible avatar animation, hidden leader motion, montage slots
  -> Damage/contact plan: notify windows, traces, target filters
  -> Feedback plan: cues, camera, rumble, UI
  -> Telemetry: accepted, rejected, interrupted, completed

The solver must track two separate truths:

  • requested intent: what direction/action the player is asking for;
  • physical result: what the pawn can actually do after collision, floor, root

motion, ledges, stairs, walls, and state constraints.

Dodge direction bugs are a symptom of mixing those truths. Player intent should be captured from raw input or a known semantic vector. Locomotion vectors that exist for animation graphs may be transformed and should not be blindly treated as gameplay intent.

#6.4 Combat motion request shape

The current FAscentiaCombatMotionRequest should evolve toward:

FAscentiaCombatMotionRequest
- Kind: Dodge, AttackRootMotion, SkillLunge, Knockback, Pull, Launch, Scripted
- SourceAbilityTag
- SourceItemTag
- IntentWorldDirection
- IntentLocalDirection
- TargetActor
- TargetLocation
- RootMotionPolicy: Ignore, Prefer, Require
- FloorPolicy: StayGrounded, AllowStepDown, FallWhenUnsupported, ForceFalling
- CollisionPolicy: SlideWorld, IgnorePawn, StopOnBlock, ProjectAlongSurface
- CancelPolicyTag
- MontageOrSequence
- MotionCurve
- MaxDistance
- MinDistance
- StartGraceSeconds
- UnsupportedFloorGraceSeconds
- MaxStepDownDistance
- MaxLedgeProbeDistance
- bAllowFallbackVelocity
- FallbackVelocity
- DebugName

This struct is a contract. GASP can consume it through Mover montage/root motion. CMC enemies can consume it through montage/root motion or launch. Future skills can consume it without hardcoding movement inside the combat component.

#6.5 Defensive state machine

Replace parallel booleans with a small FSM. Suggested first native enum:

EAscentiaDefensiveState
- Neutral
- Blocking
- GuardBroken
- ParryStartup
- ParryActive
- RiposteAvailable
- DodgeIFrame
- Staggered
- StaggerPunish
- Dead

Add a separate lightweight window model:

FAscentiaTimedCombatWindow
- WindowTag
- SourceActor
- StartServerTime
- EndServerTime
- bConsumesOnUse
- bClearsOnHit
- bClearsOnAction

Guard counter, riposte, attack-cancel, post-roll attack, jump attack landing chain, and spell cancel are all timed windows, not permanent booleans.

#6.6 Designer surface

Designers should see:

  • montage slots for each weapon family and paired mode;
  • cancel windows in DataAssets or montage notifies;
  • root-motion policy per ability/move;
  • floor/ledge policy per movement verb;
  • input buffer windows per verb;
  • per-weapon family chain definitions;
  • camera/rumble profiles;
  • debug overlays for:
  • current combat state;
  • current motion request;
  • last accepted/rejected action;
  • input intent vector;
  • selected animation;
  • floor support probe.

For GASP specifically, Class Defaults should keep:

  • Ascentia|GASP Dodge|Intent
  • Ascentia|GASP Dodge
  • Ascentia|Combat Motion|Dodge
  • Ascentia|GASP Weapon Animation
  • Ascentia|GASP Weapon
  • Ascentia|GASP Root Motion

These are useful surfaces and should not disappear into C++ obscurity.

#6.7 Validation

Combat content validation should fail or warn on:

  • attack montage has no damage-window notify;
  • dodge montage configured as root-motion-required but has no root motion;
  • root-motion attack lacks floor policy;
  • directional dodge slots missing cardinal fallbacks;
  • weapon family has no default light attack;
  • paired weapon mode has no fallback attack;
  • ability has cost but no failure UI/cue;
  • ability has cooldown but no cooldown tag;
  • GameplayCue tag missing GameplayCue prefix;
  • montage skeleton mismatch with pawn/retarget path;
  • weapon mesh has no grip transform or socket policy.

#6.8 Remote proof

The proof matrix should include:

  • gasp_dodge_motion: root motion consumed, fallback unused, BR/BL selection

correct, no scoped runtime warnings.

  • gasp_weapon_swap: greatsword start, kill, loot, sword+shield equip, LMB uses

shield-pair animation and root-motion source.

  • input_buffer: queued action fires only inside allowed window.
  • movement_attack: running/rolling/backstep variants stay isolated from standing

combo progression.

  • future combat_motion_solver: ledge, stair, wall-slide, root motion interrupt,

and cancel-window cases.

#6.9 Combat motion solver implementation contract

This section turns the current working fix set into the target architecture for every motion-locking action: attacks, dodge rolls, weapon arts, boss lunges, knockbacks, pulls, leaps, executions, hit reactions, and future skills.

#6.9.1 Core rule

Combat motion has three truths, and the solver must never confuse them:

  1. Player intent: the input/action the player is currently asking for.
  2. Authored animation intent: what the selected clip or montage wants to do.
  3. Physical result: what the capsule can do after floor, wall, ledge, collision,

movement mode, root motion, and gameplay restrictions.

Player intent chooses the move and direction. Animation expresses commitment. Physics resolves the final path. If these are collapsed into one vector, dodge direction bugs, foot sliding, ledge hovering, wall tunneling, and animation cancel oddities will keep coming back.

#6.9.2 Motion source selection

Every combat verb should declare one motion source type:

EAscentiaMotionSourceType
- FreeLocomotion
- MontageRootMotion
- ProgrammaticRootMotionSource
- MotionWarpedMontage
- LaunchOrImpulseFallback
- NoTranslationLocked

Use MontageRootMotion for authored attack steps, rolls, and heavy commits where the animation's root path is the desired feel.

Use ProgrammaticRootMotionSource for skills whose distance, arc, or target is gameplay-variable: dashes, leaps, pulls, knockbacks, lunges that depend on distance, and boss gap closers.

Use MotionWarpedMontage for target alignment: backstabs, visceral attacks, grabs, executions, mantles, vaults, or boss cinematic hits. Do not use ordinary motion warping to decide free dodge direction; the player intent vector chooses that.

Use LaunchOrImpulseFallback only as a prototype or fallback path when the move is allowed to lose its authored root-motion feel. This should be visible in telemetry. If a roll requires root motion, the fallback should warn and the content should be fixed.

#6.9.3 Intent capture

The solver samples semantic input intent continuously, independently of animation state:

FAscentiaMotionIntent
- WorldDirection
- LocalDirection
- CameraYawAtCapture
- Source: RawKeyboard, RawGamepad, EnhancedInputCache, AIRequest
- CaptureTime
- Magnitude
- ActionTag

For the player pawn, raw WASD/left-stick state should be preferred for dodge direction. Enhanced Input movement caches are useful as backup, but they may be transformed for locomotion or GASP animation graphs. They should be treated as a fallback with a maximum age and explicit axis inversion settings.

During an attack lock, the current physical velocity can be sideways, forward, or zero. That does not matter for dodge direction. If the player is pressing back-right and the dodge button is accepted, the next roll should be back-right unless design rules reject that direction.

#6.9.4 Direction bins

Ascentia uses camera-relative local input where:

Local X: left/right, right is positive
Local Y: back/forward, forward is positive

Direction slots:

F  = Y > 0, abs(X) below diagonal/cardinal threshold
B  = Y < 0, abs(X) below diagonal/cardinal threshold
R  = X > 0, abs(Y) below diagonal/cardinal threshold
L  = X < 0, abs(Y) below diagonal/cardinal threshold
FR = X > 0 and Y > 0
FL = X < 0 and Y > 0
BR = X > 0 and Y < 0
BL = X < 0 and Y < 0

This table is the canonical label convention. Asset names from content packs are not the convention. If an imported animation's visual flavor is reversed, fix the PDA_DodgeSet mapping in the Editor rather than changing gameplay signs.

Designer-facing settings:

PDA_DodgeSet
- Forward
- Back
- Left
- Right
- ForwardLeft
- ForwardRight
- BackLeft
- BackRight
- NeutralFallback
- DiagonalAxisThreshold
- DeadZone
- InputCacheMaxAgeSeconds
- bPreferRawInput
- bInvertCachedInputX
- bInvertCachedInputY
- RootMotionPolicy
- PlayRateByRollType
- IFrameWindowByRollType
- CancelInWindowTags
- RecoveryWindowTags

The current AAscentiaGaspCharacter Class Defaults are a valid MVP version of this surface. The production version should move per-moveset data into assets so AssassinGirl is not special.

#6.9.5 Floor, stairs, and ledges

Use a floor policy per motion request:

EAscentiaMotionFloorPolicy
- StayGrounded
- AllowStepDown
- FallWhenUnsupported
- ForceFalling
- IgnoreFloor

For ordinary grounded attacks, use FallWhenUnsupported. Root motion may carry the capsule past the ledge; when support is lost beyond grace and step-down thresholds, transition to falling. Do not freeze the pawn in air and do not fake ground contact beyond a real ledge.

For stairs, use AllowStepDown with thresholds:

MaxSupportedDropCm
MaxStepHeightCm
UnsupportedGraceSeconds
ProbeStartHeightCm
ProbeDistanceCm
WalkableNormalMinDot

Small downward changes within the step-down threshold are stairs or slope travel, not falling. The current attack floor guard already has the right idea: AttackRootMotionMaxSupportedFloorDrop and AttackRootMotionUnsupportedGraceSeconds prevent stair chatter while allowing a real ledge to break support.

When movement mode becomes falling, gravity owns Z. Root motion may keep contributing XY if the move's policy allows it, but Z should not pretend the character is still walking on a floor.

#6.9.6 Walls, collision, and sliding

The combat solver must move the capsule through the movement system, not by raw teleporting. Collision should be resolved by sweeps and movement-mode rules.

Policy:

EAscentiaMotionCollisionPolicy
- SlideWorld
- StopOnBlock
- ProjectAlongSurface
- IgnorePawnOnly
- NoCollisionOverride

Default dodge policy is SlideWorld: the roll tries to follow root motion, but collidable walls redirect the capsule along the surface. It should ignore the owning player's mesh and avoid self-camera collision, but it should not pass through hard world geometry.

StopOnBlock is for attacks or lunges that should visibly hit a wall. ProjectAlongSurface is for boss or skill moves that keep pressure but respect geometry. IgnorePawnOnly is for cinematic alignment where character overlap is expected but walls still matter.

#6.9.7 Cancel and commitment model

Every combat action should expose phases:

Startup
Commit
Contact
RecoveryCancelable
RecoveryLocked
Complete
Interrupted

And windows:

FAscentiaActionWindow
- WindowTag
- OpensAtNormalizedTime
- ClosesAtNormalizedTime
- AllowedActionTags
- bConsumesWindow
- bRequiresInputBuffered
- bCanSpendResourceDuringWindow

Attack-to-dodge cancellation should not be a single global float forever. The current AttackDodgeCancelDelaySeconds is the MVP control. Production should move this into action windows per weapon family and move type, with inherited defaults for each moveset.

Resource checks happen when the action is accepted. Buffered inputs can be remembered before the window opens, but should spend only when consumed.

#6.9.8 Designer surface

Build these Editor assets:

PDA_CombatMotionProfile_*
- MotionSourceType
- FloorPolicy
- CollisionPolicy
- RootMotionPolicy
- FallbackVelocityPolicy
- CancelWindows
- UnsupportedFloorGraceSeconds
- MaxSupportedDropCm
- DebugColor

PDA_Moveset_*
- WeaponFamily
- PairedMode
- LightAttackChain
- HeavyAttack
- ChargedAttack
- RunningAttack
- RollingAttack
- BackstepAttack
- JumpAttack
- GuardCounter
- DodgeSet
- DefaultMotionProfile

PDA_DodgeSet_*
- Directional animation slots
- roll-type play rates
- roll-type iframe windows
- fallback direction
- imported-pack notes

Blueprint/Editor debugging:

  • show raw input vector;
  • show cached Enhanced Input vector;
  • show selected direction slot;
  • show selected animation;
  • show root-motion consumed vs fallback launch;
  • show current floor policy and probe result;
  • show active cancel window;
  • show last rejection reason.

This is how the system stays designer-tunable instead of becoming another C++-only behavior maze.

#6.9.9 NPC and boss usage

NPCs and bosses should use the same motion request contract, but their intent source is AI:

AI goal
  -> desired target/spacing
  -> selected move
  -> motion request
  -> movement implementation consumes request

Boss attacks should not hardcode movement in the boss pawn. The Behavior Tree or ability selects a move; the move produces a motion request; the pawn-specific movement system consumes it. This keeps CMC enemies, Mover enemies, and future large bosses aligned.

Bosses need extra policies:

  • target re-acquisition during startup;
  • root-motion steering limits;
  • arena boundary clamps;
  • ledge/fall permissions;
  • wall stop vs wall slide;
  • cinematic lock for grabs;
  • multiplayer-safe authority later.

#6.9.10 Verification

The production proof suite should include:

  • neutral dodge selects forward fallback;
  • F/B/L/R/FL/FR/BL/BR each selects the correct slot by local intent;
  • back-left/back-right imported asset mapping can be swapped in PDA_DodgeSet

without code;

  • dodge during attack lock uses requested intent, not current velocity;
  • dodge slides along a wall and does not tunnel;
  • attack root motion descends stairs without false falling;
  • attack root motion loses floor at a ledge and transitions to falling;
  • root-motion-required roll fails validation when the animation has no root

motion;

  • fallback launch is reported when root motion is missing in preferred mode;
  • boss lunge and player roll consume the same request contract.

#6.10 P11 boss quality bar implementation contract

P11 is the first boss-quality phase. It should not mean "make a bigger enemy." It should establish the combat-feel kernel that every later boss, elite, weapon skill, spell, and encounter can reuse.

The player-facing quality bar:

  • the boss is readable before it is fair;
  • every hit has visible startup, contact, recovery, and punish language;
  • the arena teaches spacing and movement instead of only containing the fight;
  • phases change real behavior, not only music or a name card;
  • fog wall, intro, health bar, phase shift, defeat, reward, and persistence feel

like one authored encounter;

  • Designers can tune move selection, windows, cues, arena anchors, and phase

rules without editing native code;

  • remote proof can answer whether the boss still works after animation, motion,

cue, or AI changes.

#6.10.1 Current P11 footholds

Existing useful pieces:

  • native boss definition asset exists with display name, intro presentation,

phase rows, optional phase ability sets, music/cue state, behavior tag, and fog-wall hook;

  • Behavior Tree and Blackboard assets exist for a first boss loop;
  • remote proof scenarios already touch boss phase, boss presentation, fog wall,

stagger, resource pressure, and baseline gameplay;

  • combat motion request architecture now gives boss lunges and player rolls the

same movement language;

  • presentation architecture already expects boss bars, music states, cues,

camera feedback, and phase events;

  • P18 later expands the boss system into scalable enemy/boss production, so P11

can focus on quality bar and kernel contracts.

Remaining P11 gaps:

  • boss move selection is not yet a Designer-authored matrix;
  • boss attacks are not fully represented as ability plus montage plus motion

profile plus damage/contact windows;

  • arena anchors, fog wall, camera, phase thresholds, punish windows, and reward

state are not one encounter asset;

  • boss telemetry does not yet explain why a move was selected/rejected;
  • no dedicated boss quality dashboard card exists;
  • no reusable boss proof report schema exists.

#6.10.2 Boss encounter ownership

Boss ownership should be explicit:

PDA_BossEncounter
  BossTag
  BossDefinition
  ArenaProfile
  FogWallProfile
  IntroProfile
  PhaseProfiles
  MoveMatrix
  RewardProfile
  PersistenceProfile
  MusicStateProfile
  CameraProfile
  PresentationCueProfile
  ProofScenarioProfile

Runtime actors:

BP_AscentiaBossArena
  arena bounds
  entry gates
  anchor points
  camera volumes
  fog wall links
  respawn/checkpoint links
  debug draw

BP_AscentiaFogWall
  encounter link
  entry policy
  exit policy
  multiplayer future policy
  visual/audio cue

BP_AscentiaBossRewardSource
  reward profile
  persistence tag
  drop/spawn policy

Native orchestration:

UAscentiaBossEncounterComponent
  BeginEncounter
  EndEncounter
  ApplyPhase
  QueryMoveLegality
  RecordBossTelemetry
  BroadcastPresentationEvent
  PersistDefeat

UAscentiaBossMoveComponent or service
  select legal move
  emit ability activation request
  write selected move to Blackboard
  record rejection reasons

The boss pawn should not own the whole encounter. The arena owns arena facts, the boss definition owns boss identity, the move matrix owns selection rules, the ability owns execution, and world state owns persistence.

#6.10.3 Boss move data

Every boss move should be an authored object or row with stable identity:

PDA_BossMove
  MoveTag
  DisplayName
  AbilityClass
  Montage
  StartSection
  PhaseTags
  RangeBand
  AngleBand
  TargetStateRequirements
  BossStateRequirements
  CooldownSeconds
  RepeatPenalty
  Weight
  CostProfile
  MotionProfile
  DamageProfile
  ContactWindows
  IFrameOrArmorWindows
  RotationPolicy
  TargetReacquirePolicy
  ArenaBoundaryPolicy
  PunishWindowProfile
  CueProfile
  CameraFeedbackProfile
  AudioProfile

Selection inputs:

FAscentiaBossMoveQuery
  BossTag
  PhaseTag
  TargetActor
  DistanceToTarget
  AngleToTarget
  BossHealthFraction
  TargetHealthFraction
  TargetResourceState
  LastMoveTags
  TimeSinceLastMove
  ArenaAnchorState
  WorldStateTags
  DifficultyProfile

Selection output:

FAscentiaBossMoveSelection
  SelectedMoveTag
  LegalMoveTags
  RejectedMoveTags
  RejectionReasons
  ScoreBreakdown
  SelectedAbilityClass
  BlackboardWritePolicy
  TelemetryId

Move selection should be deterministic when given a seed for tests. Designers should be able to preview "at 800 cm, phase two, target behind boss, last move was leap" and see the legal move list.

#6.10.4 Ability and montage execution

Boss moves should execute through GAS where possible:

Boss move selected
  -> GameplayAbility activates
  -> cost/cooldown/tags commit if relevant
  -> montage starts
  -> startup window opens
  -> optional motion request begins
  -> damage/contact notify fires
  -> GameplayEffect/damage path applies
  -> GameplayCue/MetaSound/Niagara/camera fires
  -> recovery/punish window opens
  -> ability ends or transitions

Required windows:

Startup
Commit
Contact
RecoveryLocked
RecoveryPunishable
Complete
Interrupted

Required notify/cue categories:

Notify.Attack.Contact
Notify.Attack.SwingAudio
Notify.Attack.TrailStart
Notify.Attack.TrailEnd
Notify.Motion.WarpWindow
Notify.Motion.RootMotionWindow
Notify.Boss.PunishWindowOpen
Notify.Boss.PunishWindowClose
GameplayCue.Ascentia.Boss.Attack.*
GameplayCue.Ascentia.Boss.Phase.*
GameplayCue.Ascentia.Boss.Defeat.*

If a move has damage but no contact window, validation fails. If a move requires root motion but the animation has no root motion, validation fails. If a move expects Motion Warping, the montage must have named warp windows and the ability must set matching warp targets before montage play.

#6.10.5 Phase model

Phases should change at least one real system:

  • legal move set;
  • move weights/repeat penalties;
  • arena anchors or safe zones;
  • aggression/spacing target;
  • damage/status profile;
  • summon/add policy;
  • projectile or hazard policy;
  • music state;
  • camera/presentation intensity;
  • reward or persistence state after defeat.

Phase data:

PDA_BossPhase
  PhaseTag
  HealthThreshold
  EntryAbility
  EntryMontage
  EntryCue
  MusicState
  MoveSetAdditions
  MoveSetRemovals
  WeightOverrides
  ArenaRuleOverrides
  DamageProfileOverrides
  StatusResistanceOverrides
  StaggerResistanceProfile
  PresentationProfile

Phase transition rules:

  • transition once unless explicitly repeatable;
  • do not transition during invalid animation states unless the phase profile

allows interruption;

  • if phase entry has a cinematic or roar, mark whether the player can act;
  • phase state must save/replicate later through the encounter component;
  • phase change should emit one presentation event and one telemetry event.

#6.10.6 Arena and area co-design

FromSoft's Elden Ring production lesson is highly applicable: major boss gameplay forms a premise for the area, and the boss can be adjusted based on the map it appears in. Ascentia should formalize that relationship.

Arena profile:

PDA_BossArenaProfile
  ArenaTag
  BossTag
  EntryPoints
  SafeDistanceBands
  HazardZones
  PillarOrCoverPolicy
  CameraVolumes
  AnchorPoints
  LeashBounds
  FogWallLinks
  CheckpointLinks
  CorpseRunPolicy
  SummonPolicy
  ReadabilityLightingProfile

Rules:

  • every boss move with a lunge, leap, cone, beam, or area attack should be tested

against arena size and anchors;

  • fog wall should not be a separate hand-placed obstacle with no encounter link;
  • camera volumes and collision should be tested during large moves, wall slide,

lock-on, and close-range pressure;

  • corpse-run/checkpoint distance should be authored by profile rather than

accidental pawn spawn placement;

  • arena lighting/weather should support silhouette readability before beauty.

#6.10.7 Stagger, poise, punish, and critical rewards

Boss pressure needs release valves. P11 should not finish without a readable punish loop:

Player reads move
  -> avoids/blocks/parries/positions correctly
  -> boss enters recovery or stagger vulnerability
  -> player earns safe attack, riposte, resource refill, or positional advantage

Boss stagger profile:

PDA_BossStaggerProfile
  PoiseHealth
  PoiseDamageMultipliers
  DecayPolicy
  StaggerThresholds
  ImmunityAfterStaggerSeconds
  RiposteSocket
  RiposteWindowSeconds
  PunishCue
  UIStatePolicy

Punish windows should not be only hidden timing in an animation. They should emit telemetry and optional debug overlays so Designers can see whether the player actually received the promised opening.

#6.10.8 Designer surfaces and tools

P11 Editor assets:

PDA_BossEncounter_*
PDA_BossDefinition_*
PDA_BossPhase_*
PDA_BossMove_*
PDA_BossArenaProfile_*
PDA_BossFogWallProfile_*
PDA_BossStaggerProfile_*
PDA_BossRewardProfile_*
PDA_BossPresentationProfile_*
PDA_BossProofScenario_*

P11 Editor tools:

EUW_AscentiaBossMoveMatrix
EUW_AscentiaBossPhasePreview
EUW_AscentiaEncounterForge
EUW_AscentiaMotionInspector
EUW_AscentiaNotifyWindowDoctor
EUW_AscentiaHitboxReplay
EUW_AscentiaCueMatrix
EUW_AscentiaBossQualityDashboard

Runtime widgets/presentation:

WBP_AscentiaBossNameCard
WBP_AscentiaBossBar
WBP_AscentiaBossPhaseBanner
WBP_AscentiaBossDebugOverlay

Designer-visible questions:

  • Which moves are legal in this phase and why?
  • Why did the boss select this move?
  • What range/angle does this move expect?
  • Where are damage, trail, audio, camera, and punish windows?
  • Does root motion move the capsule or only the mesh?
  • What happens if the boss hits a wall, stairs, ledge, fog wall, or arena edge?
  • Which cue/audio/camera assets fire for startup/contact/recovery/phase/defeat?
  • Which validator catches this if it breaks?

#6.10.9 Validation

P11 validation should include:

  • boss encounter has boss definition, arena profile, fog wall profile, move

matrix, reward profile, and persistence profile;

  • every boss phase has a health threshold and at least one real gameplay change;
  • phase thresholds are sorted and non-overlapping;
  • every boss move has an ability, montage, phase availability, range band,

cooldown/repeat policy, damage or non-damage purpose, and presentation profile;

  • damage moves have contact windows and damage profiles;
  • root-motion-required moves have root motion enabled and extracted;
  • Motion Warping moves have named montage windows and ability warp target setup;
  • boss move cue tags exist and begin with GameplayCue;
  • swing audio/trail notifies exist for weapon or limb attacks that declare them;
  • fog wall links to encounter state and blocks/reopens according to policy;
  • boss reward profile persists defeat and cannot duplicate reward grants;
  • boss arena has anchors, bounds, entry, camera policy, and checkpoint policy;
  • boss stagger profile cannot create permanent stun loops unless explicitly

allowed in a test-only profile.

#6.10.10 Proof suite

Remote/proof scenarios:

boss_intro
  enter arena
  fog wall closes
  name card and music state fire
  boss target acquired

boss_move_matrix
  sample range/angle/phase states
  report legal moves and selected move
  verify repeat penalties and cooldowns

boss_phase
  damage boss below threshold
  phase changes once
  move set and music/presentation update

boss_contact_windows
  play selected moves
  verify swing audio, trail, damage, camera, and cue windows

boss_motion_arena
  lunge/leap near wall, stair, ledge, and arena edge
  verify collision/floor policy and no mesh/capsule divergence

boss_stagger_punish
  apply poise damage
  trigger stagger
  verify punish/riposte window and recovery

fog_wall
  enter, die, respawn, re-enter, defeat
  verify wall and checkpoint policy

boss_defeat_persistence
  defeat boss
  save/load/rest
  verify defeated state and reward grant persistence

Evidence report:

FAscentiaBossQualityReport
  BossTag
  EncounterTag
  BuildVersion
  MoveCoverage
  PhaseCoverage
  MissingWindows
  MissingCues
  MotionPolicyFailures
  ArenaPolicyFailures
  StaggerPunishResults
  FogWallResults
  PersistenceResults
  HumanFeelVerdict
  ScreenshotPaths
  ReplayPaths

P11 is complete only when the first boss can be changed by editing boss moves, phase profiles, arena profile, montage windows, cues, and reward profile, and the proof suite can show what broke.

#6.11 P11 starter implementation package

P11 should begin with one shared combat-motion contract and one boss-quality vertical slice. The goal is not to solve every weapon and boss. The goal is to prove that player dodge, player attack root motion, and one boss lunge all go through the same request/report language, while Designers can see the motion policy, montage windows, root-motion state, cue coverage, and arena failures in the Editor.

Reference posture:

  • Use animation root motion when the authored clip is the feel source: rolls,

committed attacks, heavy attacks, and boss moves with fixed spacing.

  • Use Motion Warping for target-aligned authored moments such as executions,

grabs, backstabs, synced boss hits, or ripostes. Do not use it as a hidden fix for ordinary free dodge direction.

  • Use root-motion sources or programmatic movement when distance/arc is

gameplay-variable: dashes, pulls, knockbacks, leaps, and boss gap closers.

  • Animation Montages and Notifies are the contract boundary for damage windows,

swing audio, trail start/end, cancel windows, iframes, root-motion windows, and punish windows.

  • Mover/GASP and CMC enemies can consume the same FAscentiaCombatMotionRequest

through different adapters. The combat component should not directly launch or teleport actors.

  • Root-motion-required content must fail validation when the animation does not

actually provide usable root motion.

Tools/remote_playtest.py scenarios: combat_motion_solver,
  gasp_dodge_motion, gasp_weapon_swap, combat_motion_ledge_stair,
  combat_motion_wall_slide, boss_move_matrix, boss_motion_arena,
  boss_contact_windows, boss_stagger_punish

The first runtime ownership split:

UAscentiaCombatMotionSolver : UGameInstanceSubsystem
  Validates request legality, resolves motion profile, direction slot, floor
  policy, collision policy, cancel window, and selected adapter.

UAscentiaCombatMotionComponent : UActorComponent
  Lives on combatants that can execute motion-locking actions. Tracks active
  request, active action windows, floor probes, rejection reasons, and reports.

IAscentiaCombatMotionAdapter
  Pawn-specific consumer interface. GASP/Mover, CMC characters, bosses, and
  future skills implement adapters without changing combat request semantics.

UAscentiaBossEncounterComponent : UActorComponent
  Lives on boss arena/encounter actors. Owns phase state, fog wall links, move
  matrix selection, reward/persistence hooks, and boss-quality evidence.

EUW_AscentiaMotionInspector

  cancel windows, and rejection reason.

EUW_AscentiaRootMotionPolicyLab
  Audits selected montages/sequences against motion profiles and reports
  root-motion-required clips with no usable root motion.

  Shows damage, trail, swing audio, cancel, iframe, motion-warp, root-motion, and
  punish windows on a montage timeline.

  Lets Designers sample distance/angle/phase/last-move states and see legal
  moves, rejected moves, score breakdowns, and Blackboard writes.

EUW_AscentiaBossQualityDashboard
  Aggregates move coverage, phase coverage, arena motion proof, fog wall,
  stagger/punish, persistence, cue coverage, screenshots, and human verdict.

P11 starter tickets:

  • P11-001: Add combat-motion request/result structs, solver subsystem,

component state, and GASP/CMC adapter interfaces while preserving current GASP attack and dodge behavior.

  • P11-002: Move current dodge direction data into PDA_DodgeSet_*, add

direction-selection report, and prove all eight direction bins plus neutral fallback.

  • P11-003: Add floor/collision policy structs and ledge/stair/wall-slide proof

for attack root motion and dodge rolls.

  • P11-004: Add action-window structs, notify-window audit, and light-attack

damage/trail/audio/cancel timeline proof.

  • P11-005: Add PDA_CombatMotionProfile_*, root-motion policy validation, and

root-motion-required failure proof.

  • P11-006: Add PDA_BossEncounter_DraugrWarden_MVP, arena/fog/stagger profiles,

and UAscentiaBossEncounterComponent.

  • P11-007: Add boss move query/selection, EUW_AscentiaBossMoveMatrix, and

deterministic phase/range/angle selection proof.

  • P11-008: Add boss motion arena proof for lunge/leap near wall, stair, ledge,

fog wall, and arena bounds.

  • P11-009: Add boss stagger/punish/fog-wall/defeat-persistence proof and quality

report.

  • P11-010: Wire motion inspector, root-motion lab, notify doctor, boss quality

dashboard, evidence folders, and dashboard cards.

Recommended implementation order:

  1. P11-001 through P11-003 first. The solver, direction map, and physical

floor/collision rules are the foundation for every motion-locking action.

  1. P11-004 and P11-005 next. Montage windows and root-motion validation prevent

animation content from silently breaking combat feel.

  1. P11-006 and P11-007 establish the first boss as an authored encounter with a

move matrix instead of a larger enemy pawn.

  1. P11-008 and P11-009 prove the boss in the arena, including wall/ledge/stair,

punish, fog wall, reward, and persistence behavior.

  1. P11-010 closes the loop with Designer tools and evidence.

#6.12 Animation asset production, retargeting, motion matching, and compression package

P11 also needs an animation-content pipeline. The current GASP integration proved the danger: a character can be technically possessed, input can work, and combat can fire, while the visible pawn still T-poses or silently loses animation because a retarget map, skeleton, motion database, montage, compression setting, or package dependency is wrong.

This package owns animation asset acceptance:

external animation pack / GASP sample / mocap / hand-authored clip
  -> animation staging folder
  -> skeleton/IK Rig/IK Retargeter profile
  -> retarget preview and export policy
  -> motion matching / montage / sequence classification
  -> root-motion and notify validation
  -> compression and cook dependency validation
  -> Designer acceptance and production promotion

Engine-facing posture:

  • IK Rig and IK Retargeter are the official bridge for sharing animations across

different skeletons. Ascentia should treat every skeleton pair as a named retarget profile, not an ad hoc Blueprint reference.

  • Motion Matching and Pose Search Databases are the locomotion selection layer

for GASP-style movement. They need schema/database ownership, database LOD policy, chooser selection proof, and debug captures.

  • Control Rig is the in-engine rig/animation correction layer for authored fixes,

pose cleanup, additive adjustments, and cinematic/keyframe edits.

  • Animation Montages, Slots, and Notifies remain the action/weapon/spell timing

contract. The animation pipeline should validate them before P14/P15/P18 use them.

  • Bone and Curve Compression Settings are production assets. Missing compression

settings, especially copied sample-project settings, should fail an audit before PIE or cook.

  RootMotionResults
  CompressionResults
  CookDependencyResults
  TposeRiskAssets
  PromotionPlanPath
  BlockingFailures

Promotion stages:

StagingImported
  raw pack/sample/mocap content; no gameplay references allowed

SkeletonClassified
  source skeleton, target skeleton, required chains, sockets, curves, and root
  policy are recorded

RetargetValidated
  IK Rig, IK Retargeter, chain map, retarget pose, preview clips, and exported
  target animations pass acceptance

RuntimeClassified
  asset is classified as locomotion database, montage, additive, hit reaction,
  cinematic, pose asset, or prototype-only clip

GameplayValidated
  root motion, slots, notifies, notify states, sync markers, motion matching
  database membership, and compression settings pass the owning profile

ProductionAccepted
  asset moved to production folder, package dependencies resolve in cook, and
  latest evidence links from the Designer tool

Validation rules:

  • no gameplay asset may reference /Animation/Staging;
  • every target character has a skeleton profile and retarget profile;
  • every IK Retargeter used by gameplay has a preview pose set and latest audit;
  • every root-motion-required roll, attack, lunge, or boss move must report

usable root motion after retargeting;

  • every montage used by combat/spells/enemies must have an action manifest,

slot, required sections, and required notifies;

  • every motion matching database has schema, chooser ownership, database LOD

policy, and a debug capture profile;

  • every animation asset has explicit bone and curve compression policy, or an

approved inherited project policy;

  • missing compression settings from copied sample content are package blockers;
  • additive flags, sync markers, curves, and root-lock settings are audited

before promotion;

  • a T-pose risk is blocking when the target pawn lacks a valid Anim Blueprint,

linked layer, skeleton profile, retarget profile, or required runtime database.

Designer-facing tools:

EUW_AscentiaAnimationPackAudit
  Scans GASP, Essential Bow, mocap, and marketplace animation folders for
  skeletons, IK Rigs, IK Retargeters, missing dependencies, compression settings,
  root motion, additive flags, sync markers, and notifies.

EUW_AscentiaRetargetProfileLab
  Previews source/target skeleton pairs, retarget poses, chain maps, foot plant,
  root scale, preview clips, and exported animation results.

EUW_AscentiaMotionMatchingDatabaseDoctor
  Shows schema, databases, chooser table, database LOD, selected databases by
  gameplay state, missing clips, dense/sparse coverage, and debug captures.

EUW_AscentiaAnimationCompressionDoctor
  Lists animation assets with missing bone/curve compression settings, sample
  project dependencies, memory warnings, and recompression policy.

EUW_AscentiaAnimationPromotionPlanner
  Builds a dry-run move/rename/fixup plan from staging to production folders,
  including redirector, cook, source-control, and dependency warnings.

EUW_AscentiaTposeRiskScanner
  Starts from a pawn/avatar/character preset and reports missing mesh, skeleton,
  Anim Blueprint, linked layers, retarget profile, motion database, compression
  setting, or package dependency.

Commandlet and script contracts:

UnrealEditor-Cmd.exe Ascentia.uproject -run=AscentiaAnimationPackAudit
  -AnimationRoot=/Game/Ascentia/Animation/Staging
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/AnimationPipeline

UnrealEditor-Cmd.exe Ascentia.uproject -run=AscentiaRetargetProfileAudit
  -Profile=/Game/Ascentia/Animation/Validation/PDA_RetargetProfile_UEFN_to_AssassinGirl
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/Retarget

UnrealEditor-Cmd.exe Ascentia.uproject -run=AscentiaMotionMatchingAudit
  -Profile=/Game/Ascentia/Animation/Validation/PDA_MotionMatchingProfile_GASP_Player
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/MotionMatching

UnrealEditor-Cmd.exe Ascentia.uproject -run=AscentiaAnimationCompressionAudit
  -Root=/Game/Ascentia/Animation
  -ReportRoot=Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/AnimationCompression

Evidence roots:

Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/AnimationPipeline/{RunId}/report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/Retarget/{RunId}/report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/MotionMatching/{RunId}/report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/AnimationCompression/{RunId}/report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/TposeRisk/{RunId}/report.json
Saved/AscentiaEvidence/P11/AnimationPipeline/{RunId}/screenshots/

Starter proof matrix:

AnimationPack_GASP_DependencyScan
AnimationPack_EssentialBow_DependencyScan
Retarget_UEFN_to_AssassinGirl_PreviewPoseSet
Retarget_UEFN_to_AssassinGirl_RootMotionPreserved
Retarget_PlayerCreatorBase_ProfileExists
MotionMatching_GASP_Player_DatabaseCoverage
MotionMatching_GASP_Player_ChooserCoverage
